elements.sent-2411-001: This work builds on Varchenko et al's introduction of bilinear forms for hyperplane arrangements, where the determinant of the associated matrices factorizes into simple components.
elements.sent-2411-002: While one of the determinant formula developed by Varchenko has been generalized to complexes of oriented matroids (COMs) already, this question was open for another, distinct form.
elements.sent-2411-003: Motivated by work from Varchenko and Brylawski, who generalized the alternative bilinear form and its determinant formula from hyperplane arrangements to matroids, we examine whether this formula can similarly be generalized to COMs.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our findings affirm this generalization, and we further extend the determinant formula to bouquets of geometric lattices as introduced by Laurent et al..
elements.sent-2411-005: Varchenko and coauthors introduced various bili\-near forms for hyperplane arrangements.
elements.sent-2411-006: A common feature of these forms is that the determinant of the matrix representing them can be factorized into a product with remarkably simple factors.
elements.sent-2411-007: The determinant formula in \cite{varchenko1995multidimensional has been generalized to oriented matroids (OMs) \cite{hochstattler2019varchenko .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Building on this, we extended the formula further to complexes of oriented matroids (COMs), which generalize OMs, in \cite{hochstattler2022signed .}
elements.sent-2411-009: During this work, Varchenko pointed us to his related work with Brylawski \cite{brylawski1997determinant , which explores a distinct bilinear form with a determinant formula for matroids.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The bilinear form in Varchenko and Brylawski's work was initially introduced by Schechtman and Varchenko for complex hyperplane arrangements \cite{schechtman1991arrangements .}
elements.sent-2411-011: While the matrix in \cite{varchenko1995multidimensional is indexed by the full-dimensional cells of a hyperplane arrangement, in \cite{brylawski1997determinant it is defined on the flag space of the arrangement.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: For a COM, this space corresponds to the zero sets of its covectors.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since the determinant formula in \cite{varchenko1995multidimensional could be extended to COMs, a natural question arose as to whether the same generalization would apply to \cite{brylawski1997determinant .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In this work, we affirmatively answer that question.
elements.sent-2411-015: Furthermore, we achieve a more general result by proving the determinant formula for bouquets of geometric lattices introduced by Laurent et al. in \cite{laurent1989bouquets .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In our generalization, we work with maximal chains, which may have different lengths instead of the classical flags of geometric lattices.
elements.sent-2411-017: Therefore, we refer to the matrix representing the bilinear form as the chain matrix.
elements.sent-2411-018: We begin with some basics about lattices, following \cite{wachs2006poset .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $P$ be a partially ordered set (a poset).
elements.sent-2411-020: For $x, y \in P$, the meet of $x$ and $y$, denoted $x \land y$, is a unique element in $P$ that is less than or equal to both $x$ and $y$ and is greater than any other element with this property.
elements.sent-2411-021: Similarly, the join of $x$ and $y$, denoted $x \lor y$, is a unique element in $P$ that is greater than or equal to both $x$ and $y$ and is smaller than any other element with this property.
elements.sent-2411-022: In simpler terms, having a meet means there exists a greatest lower bound for $x$ and $y$, while having a join means there exists a smallest upper bound for $x$ and $y$.
elements.sent-2411-023: A poset $P$ is called a meet semilattice (or join semilattice) if every pair of elements in $P$ has a meet (or join).
elements.sent-2411-024: For $x \neq y \in P$ with $x \leq y$, we say that $y$ covers $x$ if $x \leq z \leq y$ implies $z \in \{x, y\}$.
elements.sent-2411-026: If a poset has a unique minimal element, it is denoted by $\hat{0}$, and if it has a unique maximal element, it is denoted by $\hat{1}$.
elements.sent-2411-027: An element $a \in P$ such that $\hat{0 \lessdot a$ is called an atom.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Finally, a chain $C$ in a poset is a subset of $P$ in which every pair of elements is comparable (i.e., $C$ is totally ordered).
elements.sent-2411-029: To understand bouquets of geometric lattices, we need to define geometric lattices first.
elements.sent-2411-030: Geometric lattices are a special class of lattices that arise from the lattice of flats of a matroid, making them closely related to combinatorial geometry.
elements.sent-2411-031: A lattice is called geometric if it is.
elements.sent-2411-032: Bouquets of geometric lattices extend the classical concept of geometric lattices: \begin{Definition [\cite{laurent1989bouquets Bouquets of geometric Lattices] A poset $P$ is a bouquet of geometric lattices if $P$ is a meet semilattice in which every interval is a geometric lattice.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Just as geometric lattices are linked to matroids, bouquets of geometric lattices are closely associated with simple bouquets of matroids.
elements.sent-2411-034: These connections will be explored further in \ref{sec:bouquetsMatr .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, bouquets of geometric lattices generalize the framework of geometric semilattices that have been studied by Wachs and Walker \cite{wachs1985geometric .}
elements.figure-2411-001: {\includegraphics[scale=1]{geom.eps}} {\includegraphics[scale=1]{notgeom.eps}} \caption{Examples of a lattices with a $\hat{1}$ and a $\hat{0}$ element. The lattice to the left is geometric. The lattice to the right is not geometric, since semimodularity is not fulfilled for $x$ and $y$. It is neither a bouquet of geometric lattices.\label{fig:geom}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=1]{bouquetgeom.eps} \caption{Example of a bouquet of geometric lattices.\label{fig:bouquetgeom}}
elements.sent-2411-036: A bouquet of geometric lattices is ranked with a rank function $r$.
elements.sent-2411-037: This means every unrefinable chain from a minimal element to a fixed element $x$ has the same length $r(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-038: The M\"obius function $\mu$ of a poset is defined by \begin{equation}\mu(x,y)=0  for all x \not \le y \in P \mu(x,x) = 1 for all x \in P \mu(x,y) = -\sum_{x \leq z <y} \mu(x,z) for all x <y \in P.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\mu(x,y)=0  for all x \not \le y \in P \mu(x,x) = 1 for all x \in P \mu(x,y) = -\sum_{x \leq z <y} \mu(x,z) for all x <y \in P.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: For $\mu(\hat0,x)$ we write $\mu(x)$ and we denote the unsigned version by $\mu^+(x,y) = |\mu(x,y)|$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Crapo's beta function $\beta$ \cite{crapo1967higher of a poset element is defined by \begin{equation}\beta(x) = (-1)^{r(x)}\sum_{y:y\leq x} \mu(y) r(y).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-041: We now have all the necessary components to define the cumulated rho function, a key element in our factorization formula.
elements.sent-2411-042: Let $r_1, \dots, r_k$ denote the maximal elements of $P$ such that $x \leq r_i$ for $i = 1, \dots, k$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The cumulated rho function $\rho$ is then defined by \begin{equation}\rho_P(x) = \beta(x)\sum_{i = 1}^k\mu^+(x,r_i).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-044: In the introduction of \cite{brylawski2000mobius , Brylawski gives several interpretations of this function.}
elements.sent-2411-045: We will see a application of those formulas later in \ref{ex:formula , when we demonstrate our factorization formula on a concrete example.. [3].}
elements.sent-2411-046: In order to generalize the matroid bilinear form of Brylawski and Varchenko \cite{brylawski1997determinant , we need to look at the chains of a bouquet of geometric lattices $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-047: If $\{x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\}$ is a maximal chain of $P$ this means, there is no $y \in P$ such that $x_k \lessdot y$ and \begin{equation}C=[x_1\lessdot x_2\lessdot\dots\lessdot x_k].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-048: We denote $|C|=k$ for the size of $C$.
elements.sent-2411-049: In the following we only consider maximal chains, therefore we omit the term maximal in following.
elements.sent-2411-051: We say a chain $C=\{x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\}$ is generated by $A_k$, written $ch(A_k) = C$, if \begin{equation}\label{adj} x_i = a_1\lor \dots \lor a_i\end{equation} for all $i = 1,\dots,k$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Let us now label every element $x\in P$ with an atom $a \leq x$.
elements.sent-2411-054: We call a chain $C = \{x_1,x_2,\dots,x_k\}$ neat , if $l(x_i)\leq x_i$ but $l(x_i) \nleq x_{i-1}$ for all $i$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We call a labeling convex if for every $a < x' < x$, such that $l(x) = a$, we have $l(x')=a$.
elements.sent-2411-056: In this case a chain is neat if and only if all of its elements have distinct labels.
elements.sent-2411-057: A very natural convex labeling is the min-labeling.
elements.sent-2411-058: Here, we assume a linear order on the atoms $a_1 \prec a_2 \prec \dots \prec a_n$ and define $l(x) = a_i$, where $a_i$ is the smallest atom such that $a_i \leq x$.
elements.sent-2411-059: Let us assign names to the elements of the poset first: \begin{center \includegraphics[scale = 1]{bouquetgeom1.eps \captionof{figure {Bouquet of geometric lattices with atoms $a_1, \dots, a_5$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The neat chains are $[a_5\lessdot r_4]$, $[a_5\lessdot r_3]$, $[a_5\lessdot r_2]$, $[a_4\lessdot r_1]$ and $[a_3\lessdot r_3]$ since there all elements have distinct labels.
elements.sent-2411-061: We call the set of neat maximal chains that have $r_i$ as biggest element the neat chain family $C(r_i)$ of $r_i$.
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, let $\mathcal{C = \bigcup_{i=1 ^n C(r_i)$ be the set of all neat chains of $P$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We therefore have $C(r_1) = \{[a_4\lessdot r_1]\}$, $C(r_2) = \{[a_5\lessdot r_2]\}$, $C(r_3)= \{[a_3\lessdot r_3],[a_5\lessdot r_3]\}$ and $C(r_4) = \{[a_5\lessdot r_4]\}$.
elements.sent-2411-064: The weight of an element $x \in P$ is defined by \begin{equation}w(x) = \sum_{a_i \leq x}w_i.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: We are now ready to define the chain matrix.
elements.sent-2411-066: The chain matrix $\mathfrak{C}$ is a symmetric $\mathcal{C}\times \mathcal{C -$Matrix defined by \begin{equation}C_{C,C'} = \sum\limits_{ch(a_{i_1},\dots,a_{i_k}) = C,\, ch(a_{\sigma(i_1)},\dots,a_{\sigma(i_k)}) = C'} sgn (\sigma)\cdot w_{i_1} \dots w_{i_k}\end{equation} where $C,C' \in \mathcal{C}$ and $\sigma$ is a permutation of $\{i_1,\dots,i_k\}$, $k= |C|= |C'|$.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}C_{C,C'} = \sum\limits_{ch(a_{i_1},\dots,a_{i_k}) = C,\, ch(a_{\sigma(i_1)},\dots,a_{\sigma(i_k)}) = C'} sgn (\sigma)\cdot w_{i_1} \dots w_{i_k}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-067: We start with the third entry where $C = C' = [a_5\lessdot r_3]$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Since $C = C'$, $\text{sgn (\sigma) = 1$, so both summands have a positive sign.}
elements.sent-2411-069: For the fourth entry we see that $(a_3,a_5)$ generates $[a_3\lessdot r_3]$ and $(a_5,a_3)$ generates $[a_5\lessdot r_3]$.
elements.sent-2411-070: One sees that $\text{sgn (\sigma) = -1$, therefore we get the entry $-w_3 \cdot w_5$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: The first, second and fifth entry is zero, since the corresponding chains do not share generating tuples of atoms.. \end{Example*.}
elements.sent-2411-072: We will prove the following factorization formula \begin{Theorem [Chain Matrix Determinant for Bouquets of geometric Lattices] Let $P$ be a bouquet of geometric lattices and $\mathfrak{C}$ its chain matrix.. Then \begin{equation}det(C) = \pm \prod\limits_{x \in P} w(x)^{\rho_P(x)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Furthermore, we will link complexes of oriented matroids to bouquets of geometric lattices in \ref{sec:COMs , leading to the generalization of Brylawski and Varchenkos theorem for them as well as corrollary of our theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Very recently, in \cite{eberhardt2024intersection , Eberhardt and Mautner introduce an integer-valued intersection matrix for real affine hyperplane arrangements and, more generally, for affine oriented matroids, proving a determinant formula for it.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Similar to our work, their bilinear form is closely related to the bilinear form introduced by Brylawski and Varchenko \cite{brylawski1997determinant , and they also utilize the determinant theorem established there to prove their formula.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Exploring the connection between their results and ours would be highly intriguing, particularly in determining whether their framework could also be to COMs.
elements.sent-2411-077: In this section we will first explain the result of Brylawski and Varchenko \cite{brylawski1997determinant for matroids.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the second part we will use this result to prove \ref{main .}
elements.subsection-2411-006: The Determinant of the Chain Matrix of a Matroid
elements.sent-2411-079: One special case of bouquets of geometric lattices are the flat lattices of matroids, which are even geometric lattices, as indicated in the introduction.
elements.sent-2411-080: In order to look at the determinant of the chain matrix of that special case, let us start with the definition.
elements.sent-2411-081: The elements of $\mathcal{I}$ are called independent sets.
elements.sent-2411-082: A subset of $E$ which is not in $\mathcal{I}$ is called dependent.
elements.sent-2411-083: A matroid is called simple if it has no circuits of cardinality one or two.
elements.sent-2411-085: The rank $r(S)$ of $S$ is defined by the size of the largest independent set contained in $S$.
elements.sent-2411-086: The rank of the whole matroid $r(\mathcal{M )$ is $r(E)$.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We define a partial order on the flats of a matroid where $S<S'$ if $S \subset S'$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The poset defined on the flats of a matroid forms a geometric lattice.
elements.sent-2411-089: In fact, there is a bijection between simple matroids and geometric lattices.
elements.sent-2411-090: For the flat lattice of a matroid, the chain matrix corresponds to the bilinear form of a matroid as introduced in \cite{brylawski1997determinant .}
elements.sent-2411-091: The chains of the flat lattice of a matroid are referred to as flags, and in \cite{brylawski2000mobius , the bilinear form of a matroid is also called the flag matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Varchenko and Brylawski proved the following factorization formula for the determinant of this matrix.
elements.sent-2411-093: Then \begin{equation}det(F) = \pm \prod\limits_{K  flat of  M} w(K)^{b_K}\end{equation} where $w(K) = \sum_{e \in K w_e$ and $b_K = \beta(K)|\mu(K,\hat1)|$.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Our goal is now to use this determinant formula to prove the formula for bouquets of geometric lattices.
elements.subsection-2411-007: The Chain Matrix Determinant of a Bouquet of geometric lattices
elements.sent-2411-095: To prove theorem \ref{main we need the following lemma.}

elements.subsection-2411-008: Zero Sets of Complexes of oriented Matroids
elements.sent-2411-119: One application of our theorem can be found in the context of complexes of oriented matroids (COMs).
elements.sent-2411-120: This combinatorial structure consists of a finite groundset together with vectors containing $+, -$ and $0$ entries, so called sign vectors and was introduced as a generalization of lopsided sets and oriented matroids \cite{bandelt2018coms .}
elements.sent-2411-121: We will show that the poset on the zero sets of COMs induced by inclusion forms a bouquet of geometric lattices.
elements.sent-2411-122: Before we show that, let us introduce COMs formally.
elements.sent-2411-123: A COM can be defined by a finited groundset $E$ together with a collection of sign vectors $\mathcal{L \subseteq \{0,+,-\}^{|E|}$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Before we can formulate the axioms defining a COM, we need to define the following terms.
elements.sent-2411-125: The composition of $X$ and $Y$ is defined by \begin{equation}(X \circ Y)_e =  X_e  if  X_e \neq 0, Y_e  if  X_e = 0  \forall e\in E.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}(X \circ Y)_e =  X_e  if  X_e \neq 0, Y_e  if  X_e = 0  \forall e\in E.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-126: The zero set of $X$ is denoted by \begin{equation}z(X) = E\backslashX = \{e \in E: X_e = 0\},\end{equation} where $X$ denotes the support of $X$ \begin{equation}X = \{e \in E: X_e \neq 0\}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-127: The pair $\mathcal{M =(E,\mathcal{L )$ is called a COM, if $\mathcal{L}$ satisfies.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: Face Symmetry \begin{equation}\forall X,Y \in L: X \circ (-Y) \in L.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-129: Strong Elimination \begin{equation}\forall X,Y \in L\, \forall e \in S(X,Y)\, \exists Z \in L: Z_e=0  and \forall f \in E \setminus S(X,Y): Z_f = (X \circ Y)_f.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\forall X,Y \in L\, \forall e \in S(X,Y)\, \exists Z \in L: Z_e=0  and \forall f \in E \setminus S(X,Y): Z_f = (X \circ Y)_f.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-130: The elements of $\mathcal{L}$ are called covectors.
elements.sent-2411-131: The most common definition states that $(E,\mathcal{L )$ forms an oriented matroid (OM), if $\mathcal{L}$ fulfills.}

elements.sent-2411-133: So in order to be an OM, a COM just has to fullfil (S) additionally.
elements.sent-2411-134: Note that if a COM contain the all zero vector $\mathbf{0}$, (S) is fulfilled because of (FS).
elements.sent-2411-135: So we can define OMs in terms of COMs \begin{Definition [Oriented Matroid (OM)] Let $E$ be a finite set and $\mathcal{L \subseteq \{0,+,-\}^{|E|}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: The pair $\mathcal{M =(E,\mathcal{L )$ is called an OM, if it is a COM that contains the all zero vector $\mathbf{0}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: For a covector $X\in \mathcal{L}$, the set $F(X)=\{X\circ Y\mid Y\in\mathcal{L}\}$ is called the face of $X$.
elements.sent-2411-138: Note that $(E\backslashX, F(X)\backslash X)$ is an oriented matroid.

elements.sent-2411-144: To see that $Z$ is a meet semilattice let $X,Y \in \mathcal{L}$.
elements.sent-2411-145: For the two elements $z(X)$ and $z(Y)$ of $Z$, $z(X \circ Y)$ is a meet.
elements.sent-2411-146: So every two elements have a meet, which shows that $Z$ is a meet semilattice.
elements.sent-2411-147: Now let $\{z(X),z(Y)\}$ be an interval in $Z$.
elements.sent-2411-148: We know that $(E\backslashY, F(Y)\backslash Y)$ is an OM.
elements.sent-2411-149: Using \ref{thm:OMzero we see that everything that lies below $z(Y)$ is a geometric lattice.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Since every interval of a geometric lattice is again geometric, $\{z(X),z(Y)\}$ is geometric as well.
